description: This is a local healthcheck script connecting to host 127.0.0.1 (loopback) only, never traversing a network segment where a man-in-the-middle could intercept traffic; using plain HTTP for localhost-only healthchecks is standard practice and not a real transport-security exposure.

reqURL is built entirely from local, non-attacker-controlled values (hardcoded path "/healthcheck", local host "127.0.0.1", config-defined port, and the protocol/host/path echoed back from the health-check's own outgoing request) — there is no untrusted external input reaching this log call, so format-string injection isn't reachable here.

This is client-side AngularJS controller code, not an Express request handler; Object.assign merges static, hardcoded literal objects into $scope.query, with no user-controlled data or request body involved, so mass assignment/data exfiltration does not apply.

Object.assign is merging $scope.query with a hardcoded literal object of fixed boolean values, not user-controlled data; there is no external input flowing into the assigned properties, so mass assignment/data exfiltration does not apply.

This is client-side AngularJS code (admin.js) using Object.assign to snapshot $scope.query into localStorage, not an Express route handler merging request bodies into a server-side object; there is no server response or mass-assignment surface here, so the express-data-exfiltration rule is mis-triggered.

This is client-side AngularJS code cloning the local $scope.query object into a snapshot for a user-defined preset stored in localStorage; there's no untrusted JSON being merged into a server-side object or prototype pollution vector — the Express object-assign rule appears to be misfiring on unrelated client-side state cloning.

This is client-side AngularJS controller code (uses $scope, localStorage) with no Express server, HTTP request/response, or network boundary involved; Object.assign merges a locally-stored preset query object into $scope.query for UI filtering, not into an HTTP response, so the express-data-exfiltration/mass-assignment rule is mis-triggered here.

This is client-side AngularJS code (uses $scope, localStorage, window.prompt) in a browser admin UI, not an Express server route handler at all — there is no HTTP response being constructed here. Object.assign is just shallow-copying the local $scope.query object to save as a UI preset in localStorage, so the express-data-exfiltration rule (meant for server-side merging of untrusted input into response objects) is a misapplication/misclassification.

This is client-side AngularJS code (not Express server code), and Object.assign({}, $scope.query) merges the app's own in-memory scope object into a fresh {} that is then stored in the user's own localStorage — there's no untrusted server input or shared/prototype object being polluted. The 'object-assign-express' rule appears mis-triggered outside its intended Express req.body context.

This is client-side AngularJS controller code, not an Express server route — the express-data-exfiltration rule is mis-triggered here. p.query originates from the browser's own localStorage (written by the same user via savePreset), not from an untrusted HTTP request or server response, so there's no data exfiltration or mass-assignment path into a web response.

The code shown only guards a string-to-RegExp conversion inside a generic helper `cr()`; whether `selector` ever originates from untrusted/attacker-controlled input depends on call sites not shown here, so reachability and exploitability can't be confirmed from this snippet alone.

The regex is built from an `attribute` parameter (an HTML attribute name like href/src, part of the tool's own configuration for link rewriting), not raw arbitrary user-controlled text; the constructed patterns (`[^"]+`, `[^']+`, `[^"'][^\s>]*`) contain no nested/overlapping quantifiers and thus no catastrophic-backtracking structure even if attribute were attacker-influenced. No evidence in the shown code that `attribute` originates from untrusted network input.

The regex is built from `attribute`, a function parameter representing an HTML attribute name (e.g. "href", "src") that is virtually always supplied by the calling code, not by attacker-controlled input; the patterns themselves (`[^"]+`, `[^']+`) are linear-time with no nested quantifiers, so there's no catastrophic-backtracking risk even if attribute values vary.

The regex is built from a variable `attribute` and fixed literals (`marker`, `end`); the pattern itself has no nested/overlapping quantifiers that would cause catastrophic backtracking, and `attribute` is a function parameter that, given the surrounding code (constant `charactersAttributes` prefix), appears to be a developer-supplied attribute name list rather than untrusted external input. Without seeing the call sites, full confidence isn't possible, but nothing here indicates attacker-control

This is a local dev-proxy script that only serves static assets via GET routes (hashed filenames, static file fallback); there are no state-changing routes, session/cookie-based auth, or forms shown that CSRF protection would apply to, making the generic 'no csurf middleware' rule a mis-classification for this dev tooling context.

The dir capture is constrained to script|css, but the base capture group (.+) is unbounded and can include "../" sequences and slashes since only the trailing hash+extension pattern is anchored; path.join with this attacker-controlled base is not re-validated against PUBLIC_DIR before fs.existsSync/res.sendFile, allowing traversal to read arbitrary files outside PUBLIC_DIR (e.g. base="../../../../etc/passwd", ext chosen to satisfy \w+, and a fake matching hash).

`dir` is constrained to literal "script"/"css" by the regex alternation, but `base` (req.params[1]) comes from an unconstrained `(.+)` capture that can include `../` sequences since `.` matches `/`, and it is concatenated/joined into `filePath` with no `..`-stripping or containment check before `res.sendFile`. Real-world impact is narrowed because the full URL must still end in `.{10 hex chars}.{ext}`, limiting which files can be reached, but the code itself performs no path sanitization/validat

The `base` capture group `(.+)` matches any characters including `/` and `..` sequences, and is concatenated into `path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, dir, ...)` with no normalization or containment check (no `resolve()`+`startsWith(PUBLIC_DIR)` guard) before `res.sendFile`. Exploitability is narrowed by needing the final path segment to end in `.{10 hex chars}.{ext}`, but that's an incidental constraint from the regex, not an intentional sanitization of the traversal payload.

The `base` capture group ((.+)) is fully attacker-controlled and can contain `../` or `/` sequences, and is concatenated with an also-attacker-controlled hash/extension suffix into path.join with no normalization or containment check (no path.normalize+startsWith(PUBLIC_DIR) guard), so the resulting filePath can escape PUBLIC_DIR. Practical impact is somewhat constrained since the final path segment must still match `.<10 hex chars>.<ext>`, limiting which real files could be targeted, but the tr

The regex route captures `base` from `(.+)` in the URL path with no sanitization, and it is directly concatenated into `path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, dir, `${base}.${ext}`)`; since `dir` is a fixed enum but `base` is fully attacker-controlled and can contain `../` sequences, path.join will normalize these to escape PUBLIC_DIR, allowing arbitrary file existence checks and disclosure via res.sendFile.

base = req.params[1] comes from the greedy regex capture (.+) which permits path separators and '..' sequences, and is concatenated with an attacker-chosen extension directly into path.join(PUBLIC_DIR, dir, base+'.'+ext) before res.sendFile with no canonicalization/containment check against PUBLIC_DIR; fs.existsSync only confirms a file exists, it does not prevent traversal outside the intended directory.

opt.path is passed through a regex-escaping replace() that backslash-escapes all regex metacharacters before being interpolated into new RegExp(), so the resulting pattern is effectively a literal string match with `^`/`$` anchors — no attacker-controlled regex syntax or repetition/backtracking structure can be injected, eliminating ReDoS risk.

opt.path is escaped via a regex-metacharacter-escaping replace() before being interpolated into new RegExp(), so all regex-special characters are neutralized and the string is treated as a literal prefix match, preventing attacker-controlled regex syntax or catastrophic backtracking.

The flagged line only test-compiles the term to check regex validity (never executed against attacker data at this point), and the surrounding code explicitly checks hasCatastrophicBacktracking() and falls back to escaping the term as a literal before it's ever used for matching, which is a deliberate ReDoS mitigation rather than a vulnerability.

The regex is built from `variant.pattern` derived from `term`, but the shown code doesn't reveal where `term` originates (e.g., trusted config/CLI input from the tool's own operator vs. untrusted remote request), so reachability from untrusted input can't be confirmed. Word-boundary wrapping alone doesn't obviously introduce catastrophic backtracking, and compile failures are already caught, but runtime backtracking blowup isn't mitigated by that try/catch.

The regex pattern is built from a 'term' via termVariants()/withWordBoundaries(), but neither function's source is shown, so it's unclear whether special regex metacharacters in the term are escaped, whether the term can produce catastrophic-backtracking structure, or whether terms originate from trusted repo/config data versus untrusted external input.

repoName and branchName are interpolated unescaped into a RegExp pattern; in this anonymize-utils context these values originate from user-supplied GitHub repo/branch identifiers, and git ref syntax permits regex metacharacters like parentheses and '+' that could form a catastrophic-backtracking pattern, with no evidence of escaping or validation shown.

repoName and branchName are interpolated unescaped into RegExp source strings; since anonymous-github derives these from user-submitted GitHub URLs, an attacker can inject regex metacharacters (including catastrophic-backtracking patterns) that get compiled and later run against document content, enabling ReDoS or regex-injection rather than just a benign path match.

repoName and branchName are interpolated into RegExp source without escaping regex metacharacters, and since this is an anonymization tool that processes arbitrary submitted GitHub repos, the branch/repo name (which can legally contain regex metachars like '(', ')', '+') is attacker-influenced; a crafted branch name could inject a catastrophic-backtracking pattern that later runs against document content.

repoName/branchName are interpolated into simple non-nested regex patterns with no repetition structure that could cause catastrophic backtracking, and GitHub repo/branch names are constrained by GitHub's own naming rules (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, dots, slashes) which exclude regex metacharacters like *, +, (, ), | needed to construct an exploitable ReDoS pattern; the rule fires generically on any non-literal RegExp() call without evidence that the interpolated values can actually int

This path.join builds a logical repository tree path (parentPath + file.path) from GitHub's tree API response, used to track nested tree structure in-memory (pushed into `subtrees` for recursive traversal), not to access the local filesystem; no fs read/write using this joined path is shown.

The path.join here constructs a virtual repository-tree path (parentPath field used for tracking GitHub tree entries) from GitHub API response data, not a filesystem path used to read/write local files; no evidence in the shown code that this joined value is passed to fs operations, so it doesn't demonstrate actual path traversal to the local file system.

The path.join here only builds an in-memory `parentPath` string tracking position in a virtual GitHub tree (from the GitHub API's tree listing), not an actual filesystem read/write call; no sink here touches the local disk, so exploitability can't be confirmed from this code alone.

path.join here only builds a virtual path string used to populate a FileModel's name/path fields via basename/dirname; the result is never passed to a filesystem read/write API, so even attacker-controlled elem.path values cannot cause traversal to real files.

The result of path.join is only used to derive dirname/basename for populating a FileModel object (likely for in-memory/DB representation of a GitHub repo tree), not for any actual filesystem read/write or file access call — so there's no path traversal sink here even if elem.path is attacker-influenced.

The path `p` is passed to `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating an explicit traversal-guard check runs prior to path construction; without seeing `assertSafePath` fail to validate, this looks like the intended mitigation for CWE-22 rather than an unguarded sink.

The path parameter is passed through this.assertSafePath(p) immediately before being used in path.join, indicating explicit validation against traversal is performed; the implementation of assertSafePath is not shown but its name and placement strongly suggest it is the intended sanitization gate for this exact input.

The code calls `this.assertSafePath(p)` before using `p` in `join()`, indicating an intentional traversal guard, but the implementation of assertSafePath is not shown, so its correctness/completeness cannot be verified from the given context.

Every call site passes the user-controlled path through `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating an explicit traversal guard is applied consistently prior to path construction; the guard's implementation isn't shown, but its presence and naming strongly suggest this is exactly the traversal mitigation the rule is checking for.

The path is validated via `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before being passed to `join`, indicating an explicit guard against traversal; the tool's static pattern match doesn't see this validation. Without the implementation of assertSafePath we can't fully confirm it is sufficient, but the call site pattern strongly suggests this is intentionally sanitized rather than an unguarded sink.

The path is validated by `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before every `join()` call (send, read, fileInfo), indicating a deliberate traversal guard is applied to user input prior to path construction; without evidence that assertSafePath is ineffective, this matches the sanitized-input false-positive pattern rather than a real defect.

The code calls this.assertSafePath(path) immediately before the join(), indicating explicit path validation/sanitization occurs prior to the file system operation; the tool's static pattern match doesn't account for this guard.

The `path` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(path)` immediately before being used in `join()`, matching the same guarded pattern used in the `read` method above (lines 48-49). The tool's static pattern match doesn't recognize the custom `assertSafePath` validator as sanitization, but its presence indicates traversal is checked prior to path construction.

The user-controlled path `p` is passed through `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before being used in `join()`, strongly suggesting an explicit traversal guard is applied at the boundary; without seeing assertSafePath's implementation we can't be fully certain it's sufficient, but its name and placement indicate intentional mitigation rather than an unguarded sink.

The user-controlled path `p` is passed through `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating validation/sanitization occurs prior to the flagged call; the tool likely didn't account for this guard.

The `dir` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit validation against path traversal prior to the flagged call. Without seeing assertSafePath's implementation we can't be fully certain it's sufficient, but the naming and consistent guard pattern across sibling methods (rm/mk/write) strongly suggests this is a validated, non-exploitable path construction.

The code calls `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating an explicit guard intended to reject path-traversal sequences in `dir` prior to path construction; without seeing `assertSafePath`'s implementation this can't be fully confirmed, but the presence of a dedicated validation call directly preceding the flagged sink strongly suggests this is a sanitized, intentional pattern rather than a genuine vulnerability.

The code explicitly calls `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before the `join()` call on the same `dir` parameter, indicating a dedicated validation guard against path traversal is applied prior to path construction; the pattern mirrors the `rm` method above it which uses the identical guard-then-join structure.

The `dir` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit path-traversal validation occurs prior to the flagged call; the same guard pattern is used identically in the adjacent `rm` method. Without seeing `assertSafePath`'s implementation we can't be fully certain it's robust, but its name and placement strongly suggest this is the intended sanitization the tool failed to recognize.

The code explicitly calls `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating deliberate validation of the untrusted `dir` parameter prior to path construction; without the assertSafePath implementation showing a bypassable check, the visible code suggests traversal is guarded against rather than exploitable.

The code calls `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating the user-controlled `dir` parameter is validated/sanitized against traversal before being used in the path join; the tool's generic pattern match doesn't recognize this custom guard.

`dir` is validated by `assertSafePath(dir)` before use, and `file` in the loop comes from `fs.promises.readdir(fullPath)` (actual directory entries on disk), not from user-supplied path segments, so there is no attacker-controlled path traversal input into this `path.join` call.

The `file` values come from `fs.promises.readdir(fullPath)`, i.e., actual directory entries on disk, not attacker-supplied path strings, so they cannot contain traversal sequences like `../`; the `dir` parameter that is user-influenced is already validated via `assertSafePath(dir)` before being joined.

The `file` segment appended via `join(dir, file)` comes from `fs.readdir` enumerating an already-validated directory (line 165 `assertSafePath(dir)`), not directly from attacker-controlled path input; the recursion only descends into real filesystem entries under a checked root, so there's no traversal vector here.

The `dir` argument is validated via `this.assertSafePath(dir)` at function entry (line 165) before being used in `join`, and `file` originates from `fs.promises.readdir(fullPath)` — actual directory entries on disk rather than raw attacker-supplied path strings. The recursive call at line 176 re-validates `dir` on each invocation via the same guard, so arbitrary `../` traversal strings from external input are rejected before reaching `join`.

`file` here is a directory entry returned by the filesystem's own readdir enumeration (not raw attacker-supplied path text), and `dir` is the already-walked relative path being tracked for display; the join result is only used to populate a metadata field for `opt.onEntry`, not to open or read an arbitrary file outside the walked tree, so there's no traversal sink here.

`file` comes from enumerating actual filesystem entries returned by `fs.promises.stat`/directory listing (not attacker-supplied path text), and the joined value at line 181 is only used to populate a metadata `path` field in a callback record, not to open or read a file — the real filesystem access happens earlier via `filePath = join(fullPath, file)` using a directory that's already been recursively scoped, not raw user input.

The path `p` is passed through `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit validation guards against traversal prior to the join call; the zip entry names are separately sanitized via sanitizeZipEntryName for zip-slip protection.

The path `p` is passed through `this.assertSafePath(p)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit validation against traversal occurs prior to the join; the zip entry names extracted during the archive walk are separately sanitized via `sanitizeZipEntryName`.

The flagged `join(p, "___IGNORE___")` call uses a hardcoded literal string, not user-controlled input; the actual entry names from the zip are sanitized via `sanitizeZipEntryName` before use, and traversal segments are stripped to prevent zip-slip.

The `dir` parameter is passed to `this.assertSafePath(dir)` on the line immediately preceding the flagged `join()` call, indicating explicit path-traversal validation occurs before the join; the tool likely didn't account for this guard function.

The `dir` parameter is explicitly validated via `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before being used in `join()`, and `repoId` is passed through `this.repoPath()` rather than used raw, indicating the traversal-prone inputs are checked prior to the flagged line.

The `path` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(path)` before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit validation against path traversal prior to the flagged call; without evidence that assertSafePath is a no-op, this should not be treated as an unsanitized traversal sink.

The code explicitly calls `this.assertSafePath(path)` at line 57 before the path is used in `join()` at line 66, indicating deliberate validation against path traversal prior to the flagged sink; without evidence that assertSafePath is insufficient, this is best classified as sanitized input.

The `dir` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit path-traversal validation occurs prior to the flagged call; the function name strongly implies exactly this class of check, though the validator's implementation isn't shown.

The `dir` parameter is validated via `this.assertSafePath(dir)` on line 83 immediately before it is used in `join()` on line 86, indicating the traversal-relevant input is sanitized/validated prior to use as the semgrep rule suggests.

The code explicitly calls `this.assertSafePath(path)` immediately before using `path` in the `join()` call, indicating a dedicated validation step guards against path traversal; without seeing malformed input bypassing that check, this looks like an intentional sanitization gate rather than an unvalidated join.

The code calls `this.assertSafePath(path)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating explicit validation of the user-supplied path prior to its use in constructing the S3 key, which is the standard mitigation for this exact pattern.

The user-controlled `path` is passed through `this.assertSafePath(path)` before being used in the `join()` call, indicating explicit path-traversal validation occurs prior to the flagged line; the tool's dataflow rule doesn't account for this sanitizer.

The code calls `this.assertSafePath(path)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating an explicit path-traversal guard is applied to the user-controlled `path` argument prior to use; assuming that guard is implemented correctly, the join is safe. Confidence is not higher only because the implementation of `assertSafePath` is not shown.

The code calls `this.assertSafePath(path)` on line 168 immediately before the `join()` call on line 171, indicating the path is validated/sanitized against traversal before being used in the join. Semgrep's pattern-based rule cannot see this custom validation and flags the join call regardless.

The `path` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(path)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit path traversal validation occurs prior to the flagged line; assuming assertSafePath performs its intended job (e.g., rejecting '..' segments), this mitigates the reported traversal risk.
path is validated by this.assertSafePath(path) at line 168 before any use of join() on lines 171/177, and repoId is a server-controlled parameter, not raw user input joined without checks.

The path parameter is validated via `this.assertSafePath(path)` at line 168 before any use, and the flagged join() at line 177 merely rebuilds the same key for an error message string (not a new filesystem/S3 access) — it reuses the already-validated path used in the actual GetObjectCommand at line 171.

The `path` parameter is joined directly into the S3 object Key without visible sanitization, so an attacker-controlled path containing `../` segments could let `join()` escape the intended `repoPath(repoId)` prefix and read/overwrite objects belonging to other repos in the same bucket.

The S3 object Key is built by joining a per-repo prefix with a caller-supplied `path` with no visible sanitization; if `path` contains `../` segments, `join()` will normalize them and can produce a key that escapes the intended repoPath prefix, allowing reads/writes to other repos' objects in the same bucket (a path-traversal analogue in the S3 key namespace) unless validation exists upstream, which isn't shown here.

The `dir` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(dir)` (line 258) before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit validation against traversal sequences prior to use; without evidence assertSafePath is ineffective, this looks like sanitized input rather than a real vulnerability.

The `dir` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(dir)` on line 258 before being used in `join()` at line 266, indicating an explicit validation guard against path traversal is applied prior to the sink; without seeing assertSafePath's implementation we can't be 100% certain it's sufficient, but its name and placement strongly suggest it's the traversal-prevention check.

The `path` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(path)` on line 297 before being used in `join()` on line 303, indicating validation against traversal happens prior to use; the zip entry names are also separately sanitized via `sanitizeZipEntryName`, suggesting deliberate traversal defenses are in place.

The `path` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(path)` immediately before being used in `join()`, indicating explicit traversal validation occurs prior to use; additionally the join result is used as an S3 object-key prefix, not a local filesystem path, limiting the CWE-22 impact even if validation were incomplete.
The `dir` parameter is validated via `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before being used in the `join()` call, indicating explicit path-traversal sanitization is already applied at the boundary.

The `dir` parameter is passed through `this.assertSafePath(dir)` immediately before the `join()` call, indicating explicit validation/sanitization of the path input prior to use. Additionally, this constructs an S3 `Prefix` for a `listObjectsV2` API call, not a filesystem path, so even a residual traversal sequence would not access arbitrary filesystem files as the CWE-22 class implies.

repoPath() joins repoId directly into a filesystem path, but the surrounding comment indicates validation of path components is intended to happen before joining ('load-bearing defence') — the shown snippet doesn't include that validation logic or repoId's origin, so reachability and sanitization can't be confirmed from this code alone.

The result of path.join(req.baseUrl, req.url) is only used to build a string value logged via logger.info; it is never passed to a filesystem API (fs.readFile, res.sendFile, etc.), so there is no path-traversal sink here despite the tainted input reaching path.join.

Although path.join is called with attacker-influenced req.baseUrl/req.url, the result is only used as a string value in a log message (url field), never passed to any filesystem API — there's no file access, so path traversal isn't possible here.

The route regex's `(.+)` capture group for `base` can include path separators and `..` sequences (regex `.` matches any non-newline char), and this value is joined into the filesystem path via `join("public", dir, ...)` with no check that the resolved path stays under `public/`; `existsSync`/`sendFile` then operate directly on the unsanitized path, allowing traversal outside the intended directory as long as the requested URL still ends in a 10-hex-char segment plus extension to satisfy the rest

base = req.params[1] comes from the regex group (.+), which matches slashes and dot-dot sequences, and is concatenated with ext (the hash is discarded from the actual path used) into join("public", dir, `${base}.${ext}`) with no containment check before existsSync/res.sendFile — an attacker can supply a fake 10-hex-char segment plus ../ sequences in base to make sendFile resolve and serve files outside the public directory.

The route regex only fixes the `dir` segment to "script"|"css"; the `base` capture group `(.+)` is unconstrained and can contain "../" sequences (regex `.` matches `/`), while `ext` is dropped-hash-stripped and reattached via string concatenation. join("public", dir, `${base}.${ext}`) performs no containment check (unlike express.static's built-in protections), so a request like `/script/../../../../package.json0123456789.json`-style crafted path can escape the public directory and read arbitrar

The regex's (.+) capture group for `base` matches across `/`, and the constructed path `${base}.${ext}` discards the hash entirely, so an attacker can send `/script/../../../../etc/passwd.aaaaaaaaaa.conf` making base="../../../../etc/passwd" and ext="conf"; join() collapses the traversal, escaping the `public` directory, and existsSync only gates on file existence, not on staying within the intended directory, so any existing file with a matching extension can be disclosed via sendFile.

base = req.params[1] comes from the regex's greedy (.+) group, which can contain "../" sequences that path.join does not strip, and the code never verifies the resolved path stays under "public" before calling existsSync/sendFile; exploitability is narrowed (not eliminated) by the requirement that the target end in .<10-hex>.<ext>, but that's an exploitability constraint, not a sanitizer, so the traversal is real, if limited to filenames matching that suffix pattern.

The regex capture `base` (req.params[1]) matches `(.+)` which permits path separators and `../` sequences, and is joined into `filePath` via `join("public", dir, base+ext)` then passed to res.sendFile without any check that the resolved path stays within the public directory; existsSync only confirms a file exists, it does not prevent traversal. Since Express does not normalize `..` segments before route matching, an attacker can request a URL like `/script/../../../some/file.json` (matching the

req.params[1] ("base") is captured by an unconstrained (.+) that can contain "../" and slashes since regex dot matches any character, and only base+ext (not the hash) are used to build filePath via join("public", dir, ...); join()/resolve() do not confine the result back under "public", so a request like /script/../../../../src/server/index.aaaaaaaaaa.js lets an attacker read arbitrary files outside the intended public directory that existsSync happens to find.

The route regex only anchors `dir` to literal 'script'/'css', but the greedy `(.+)` capturing `base` is unconstrained and can contain '../' sequences (the `.` in the regex matches slashes and dots), so `join("public", dir, `${base}.${ext}`)` can escape the public directory before the existsSync/sendFile call. Practical impact is narrowed by the requirement that the resolved path still end in a `<10-hex>.<ext>`-shaped suffix that must exist on disk, but the traversal itself is unsanitized and att

Object.assign here merges a server-derived date-range filter into a MongoDB query filter object, not into a document being persisted or a response payload; the actual CSV response is built from an explicit allowlist of fields (username, email, status, isAdmin, repoCount, dateOfEntry), so no uncontrolled attribute merge reaches the client or a write operation.

res.send() is called with the result of gist.toJSON(), an object, so Express serializes it as JSON with application/json content-type rather than writing raw HTML/user-controlled markup into an HTML response; there's no reflected script execution context here, making this a mis-classification of a JSON API response as an XSS sink.

res.send() here serializes a JSON object (pullRequest.toJSON()), not an HTML/script context; sending JSON via res.send() does not execute as HTML in the browser unless a consumer later injects it unescaped into a DOM, which is outside this handler's control. This is a generic pattern match on res.send with dynamic data, not an actual reflected-HTML XSS sink.

repo.readme() returns raw content from a user-supplied/anonymized repository (untrusted data) and is passed directly to res.send(), which defaults to Content-Type text/html for string bodies; if the README contains HTML/script markup it will be rendered unescaped in the browser, enabling stored XSS.

The redirect target's host is hardcoded to https://gist.github.com/; only the path segment (gist.source.gistId) is variable, so an attacker cannot redirect users to an arbitrary external domain, which is the defining risk of CWE-601.

The redirect target's host is hardcoded to `https://github.com/`; the user-influenced values (repositoryFullName, pullRequestId) are only interpolated into the URL path, not the authority/host component, so an attacker cannot redirect to an arbitrary external domain.

The redirect target's scheme and host (`https://github.com/`) are hardcoded; only a path segment is filled from `repo.model.source.repositoryName`, which cannot change the destination domain to an attacker-controlled one. This does not meet the open-redirect pattern of user-controlled full URL/host.

The flagged line just wraps `body` in doctype/html/head/body tags; `body` itself is built with `escapeHtml(filePath)` and `escapeHtml(c.name)` for displayed text, and `encodeURI(c.name)` for the href attribute (which encodes quotes), so the user-controlled values reaching this template are already sanitized before concatenation.

The variable interpolated into the HTML template (`body`) is the output of `sanitizeHtml(...)`, an explicit HTML-sanitization step applied to the rendered markdown before insertion, which is exactly the mitigation the tool suggests; absent evidence that `sanitizeOptions` is misconfigured to allow scripts/event handlers, this is sanitized user input, not raw HTML.

The redirect target is always the hardcoded literal string "/w" concatenated with req.url, so the resulting Location always begins with the fixed "/w" prefix and stays a relative, same-origin path rather than an absolute or protocol-relative URL to an attacker-controlled domain; there is no way for user input to override the scheme/host, so this is not an exploitable open redirect.
